lesson.menu_title: Under Fire -- Defending Your Project in the Steering Committee
lesson.menu_desc: Straight answers under attack: own it, prove it, stay calm -- 30 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 20 -- Under Fire -- Defending Your Project in the Steering Committee
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Diogo Leal | Aula 20 -- Under Fire
phases: The Room Is Not the Enemy

plan_row.tema: Under Fire -- Defending Your Project in the Steering Committee
plan_row.foco: Straight answer first (Yes / No / I do not know) + That is on me + I will send it by 5 PM
plan_row.atividade: Hot-seat card + role-play with a committee looking for someone to blame
plan_row.homework: Record a 2-min defence: the straight answer, what you own, the number, the plan

reading.rtitle: The Room Is Not the Enemy
paras: In a steering committee, the questions sound like attacks, and the first instinct is to defend yourself. That instinct is the enemy. The moment you start explaining before you answer, the room decides you are hiding something.

So answer first. Yes, no, or a number -- one sentence, and then the reason. If you do not know, say so, and give the hour you will send it: 'I do not have that number now. You will have it by five.' An honest 'I do not know' costs you nothing. A guess that turns out wrong costs you everything.

And when the mistake is yours, own it in four words -- 'that is on me' -- then move immediately to what you are doing about it. Nobody in that room is looking for a perfect project. They are looking for somebody who is accountable, calm, and impossible to surprise.

choices: Explain the context first, so the committee understands the difficulty.
Answer first -- yes, no, or a number -- own your mistakes, and never guess.
Never admit a mistake in front of directors.

items: You should explain the background before you answer the question.
Answer first: yes, no, or a number. Then the reason.
"I do not know -- you will have it by five" is a strong answer.
An honest "I do not know" costs nothing. A wrong guess costs everything.
When the mistake is yours, you should own it and move to the plan.
'That is on me' -- four words, then what you are doing about it.

bank.label: The five lines of a defence
items: Yes. The go-live moved twice, and I am accountable for the date.
The reason is the database approval -- eleven days of silence, and four weeks last year.
The first delay is on me: I should have escalated a week earlier.
I do not have that number now. You will have it by five o'clock.
I stand by June 27th, and here is what protects it.
If you want a different date, I need one of two things -- and I can explain both.

items.situation: "Whose fault is this?" Answer in one sentence.
tips: Own the part that is yours.

items.situation: Own your mistake in four words -- then move.
tips: Then: what you are doing about it.

items.situation: They ask for a number you do not have.
tips: 'I will not guess it in this room.'
Give an hour, and keep it.

items.situation: "Is your team slow?" Answer without attacking anyone.
Defend the new date in one sentence.
tips: 'I stand by June 27th, and two things protect it.'

items.situation: Ask the room for the one thing you need.
tips: Never leave the hot seat empty-handed.
Say what you cannot protect without it.

pressure.title: Answer live
items: Should we replace you as project manager?
You said June 20th, then June 27th. Why should we believe this date?
The client says your team never answers. Is that true?

pressure.text: Answer first, own second, prove third. Steady voice. Never guess a number, and never blame a person who is not in the room.

listenings.text: Diogo, twenty minutes, three directors, and I want you to hear this before you walk in. They are not looking for a perfect project. They are looking for a name, so that somebody in this building is responsible. If you defend yourself, they will smell it, and it will take an hour. So do the opposite. Answer the question in one sentence, before you explain anything. Own the part that is yours, in four words, and then move to the plan. If they ask for a number you do not have, do not invent it -- give them an hour, and send it. And whatever happens, keep your voice steady. The calm person owns that room, and after twenty minutes they will not remember your slides. They will remember whether they can trust you.

Yes. The go-live moved twice, and I am accountable for the date. The reason is the database approval: the client's security team went silent for eleven days, and the same approval took four weeks last year. Now, the part that is on me: I should have escalated a week earlier. I did not, and that first week is mine. Here is where we are. I stand by June 27th, and two things protect it: the tests are already running on the old database, and the scope is now the standard reports only. You asked what the delay costs. I do not have the final number in front of me -- I will not guess it in this room. You will have it by five o'clock today. And there is one thing I need from this committee: the client's security team on a call this week. Without that call, I cannot protect June 27th, and I would rather tell you now than in September.

extra_audio.text: The committee asks whose fault the delay is. Diogo answers straight, and says the first week is on him. He stands by June 27th, and names the two things that protect it. When they ask for a number he does not have, he refuses to guess and promises it by five o'clock. Finally, he asks the room for the client's security team on a call this week.
